There was indeed K-Pop, and demons were hunted, that much is true. As a concrete work, this reminded me more than anything of Frozen, albeit with a lot more life pumped into its comedic elements and visual properties. As a rule I generally prefer musical films in which the numbers are more integrated into the story and the actions of the characters, rather than just a break from any sort of narrative tension with some tenuously connected lyrics. But for what it is, it's pleasant, engaging and very funny. You could certainly do a lot worse.

author: Unknown
text:
Stan Huntr/x. Believe the hype. This movie is a blast.
KPop Demon Hunters is vibrantly animated and brimming with punchy action sequences and infectious earworms (will “Golden” ever leave my head? Only time will tell). The story is refreshingly simple, which in and of itself is a feat given how unruly this mash-up concept could easily become. Some plot points are clunky and there are underdeveloped character arcs, but this never really feels like it’s to the detriment of the film. It’s certainly not the best animated movie as of late, but it’s probably the most fun one I’ve watched in recent memory.

text:
The fact that about 5 grown adults (2 of which are older than me) heralded Kpop Demon Hunter as the best movie they've seen all year is just straight up wild to me.
It's a simple, animated tale of self-acceptance and self-expression stretched out to 96 minutes. To be frank, it's just straight-washed pride.
The songs are catchy but shallow, the acting leaves much to be desired, the animation is good but not great, the pacing is very rushed, and the humor never lands.
For all the praise this movie recieves, I can think of several examples that far exceed it.

A vibrant, anime-inspired romp where a K-pop girl group doubles as demon slayers, battling a demonic boy band to seal a magical barrier.
Catchy earworms like "Golden" and "Takedown" fuel high-energy fight scenes, while the fluid animation bursts with cool-toned flair and infectious joy.

Can't wait for Netflix to milk the shit out of this, because they should.

Dont miss it for the world.
What a fun movie, maybe a little overhyped, but entertaining nonetheless.
The animation is stunning, the voice acting is so good, the writing is pretty clever, and that damn soundtrack is fire. Banger after banger. Give them their deserving Oscar already.
The story is nothing groundbreaking, the villain is very underwhelming, and the demons design wasn't that creative, but I get the hype.
Haven't seen many animated movies from this year, but I wouldn't be upset if this one gets the gold.

Okay how is this good? It just is.
I don't care one bit for anime nor K-Pop, but this movie is just energetic, fluffy fun. It plays like Buffy vs Backstreet Boys of Doom, it's animation is somewhere between Power Puff Girls and Mitchell's vs the Machines, with lots of creative ideas and anime tropes referenced. It makes fun of the genre of while celebrating it with catchy songs and great action scenes. Great likeable characters and timing. Cute guys, funny girls. And that derp tiger is adorable.
Yeah it's a bop.

text:
Now from someone who doesn’t know much K-Pop, and the furthest I can go with my K-Pop intelligence is fucking BTS, I really really loved this film. 
A truly triumphant piece in magic realism as KPop Demon Hunters provides an exquisitely original narrative, conjuring up some beautiful music and visuals that never feel lazy, artificial or mundane. Just a bit more of an easier landing regarding the lore and prologue and we would have a serious contender for best animated feature. 
But, honestly, I’m really surprised with how much of a good time I had with KPop Demon Hunters.

Todo el plan, plan, plan
Tremenda sorpresa por parte de Netflix.
Divertida, fresca, electrizante con su creativa animación, así como con sus grandes temazos.
Creí que por los comentarios que ví en redes, el doblaje me arruinaría la experiencia, pero no, supieron doblar muy bien las canciones.
Las Guerreras K-Pop es una gran película animada, es entretenida de ver, tiene grandes canciones, una animación fascinante que combina diferentes estilos y diferentes puestas de colores; y un trío de protagonistas muy carismáticas.
Espero no ser el único que cada que las Huntrix se movían, su animación le recordaba a estas figuras.

author: Unknown
text:
2025 Catch Up 3/4.
This is exactly the kind of chaotic genre mashup I didn’t know I needed, a neon-drenched fever dream where glitter, group choreography, and demonic possession collide in gloriously over-the-top fashion. Imagine if Blade joined forces with The Exorcist at a Seoul music festival, and you’re halfway there.
The third act loses some steam as it tries to juggle too many plot threads, maybe if the movie was a bit longer it could have fleshed stuff out better, but it’s a wild ride nonetheless.

Analytical Review
KPop Demon Hunters is a genre-blending spectacle that fuses the polish of K-pop idol culture with the mythic stakes of supernatural anime. Directed by Chris Appelhans and Maggie Kang, the film is a riot of color, choreography, and character—a high-energy animated action-musical that’s as emotionally grounded as it is visually explosive. It’s a love letter to performance, identity, and the power of girl groups who fight demons on stage and off.

author: Unknown
text:
Vi esta película por la razón más random y casual del mundo, pero no me arrepiento, ya que resultó ser una experiencia muy entretenida.
La historia gira en torno a un grupo femenino de K-pop que, aprovechando su fama, actúa como cazadoras encubiertas para proteger al mundo de demonios que lo acechan.
Aunque la premisa suena bastante bizarra y poco convencional, sorprendentemente funciona. La animación está muy bien lograda y el humor es efectivo, especialmente si estás familiarizado con el mundo del K-pop y la cultura coreana.
Es una propuesta divertida y original, con buena energía, y canciones muy pegadizas.

text:

text:
I love being pleasantly surprised by something I initially figured wouldn't be my thing.
The majority of media released nowadays is connected to an already well-known franchise, so it's nice to still get original movies that will almost certainly start their own franchise. KPop Demon Hunters has spectacular animation that is filled with stunning visuals and personality, and there is no other animated film that looks or moves like it. The voice acting is excellent, the story is compelling, and the music is EPIC. I had so much fun with this, and I look forward to seeing this become its own hit franchise.

text:
Eu já entrei pronto pra falar mal, juro. Eu já tava com o discurso ensaiado: “lá vem mais um drama plastificado com dancinha e choro de CGI.” Mas aí o filme me surpreendeu. simplesmente muito bom.
O filme é lindo, vibrante e totalmente sem vergonha de ser dramático pra cacete. As meninas entregam carisma, suor, lágrimas e umas performances que eu assistiria fácil num palco real. Cada cena parece um clipe, mas com coração, e isso é raro demais.
Tem amizade verdadeira, tem superação, tem rivalidade boa e tem um final que me fez bater palma sozinho parecendo um idiota.

author: Unknown
text:
After the passing of his wife, C. S. Lewis famously wrote “feelings, feelings, feelings. Let me try thinking instead”. 
Lewis goes on to write that even the strongest, most intimate feelings can be deceitful, an echo of the Scriptures he so enjoyed.
Even though Hollywood has adapted Lewis’s works to the big screen before, the industry went full steam ahead in the opposite direction: my feelings dictate your truth. 
Which is why it’s so refreshing to see an animated work that dares to suggest we’re no better than demons when we let emotion alone dictate the course of our lives.

Weird how this came out the same year as Sinners.

Surprisingly not terrible, but this is very far from my sort of thing. Essentially, an inclusive children's movie that we've seen so many times in different styles. Here it's... KPop? Yeah, whatever. Still, through it's utter generics, there are still a few redeeming qualities. The animation is gorgeous, and one or two of the songs went a little hard, but this is not something I'd usually turn on. Either way, I don't really care about any of this. Perfectly tolerable, I guess.

text:
While at times this feels like there was more of a consideration for catchy songs and aesthetics rather than plot, it's pretty captivating nonetheless. The central concept, that a pop group have to kill off demons is fairly out there anyway, but decent characters and those aforementioned tunes really carry it along. There is a feeling in the third act that the odds are perhaps too insurmountable, particularly as we draw closer and closer to the denouement, but it's actually wrapped up well. It looks like Netflix is the place to catch these unique, innovative, good looking, animation projects.

The animation is full of those quirky stylings and weird frame rates that Spider-Verse has inflicted on the genre now, but I can look past most of that. A bit overrated, but I liked more than I might like to admit.

text:
The little Netflix movie that could.
KPOP was part of a 2021 post-pandemic deal between Sony and Netlfix where Sony would develop and produce, and Netflix would cover the full budgets plus a 25%-of-budget premium in exchange for all rights worldwide upon release. KPOP had a $100M budget - small by mainstream animation standards where most Pixar features are $250M. Sony was paid $100M + $25M by Netflix.
To the streaming service's credit, they took KPOP out to toy companies,… more

KPop Demon Hunters (such a silly title) is a surprisingly wholesome, vibrant, and heartfelt animated flick about living with shame and self-loathing that I’m so glad I gave it a shot. A somewhat generic story with a lot of familiar plot points and character development that’s nonetheless energetic, heart-thumping, and so wonderfully spirited in its colorful animation. I guess we’ll get an announcement for a sequel any day now given how well it does, but I’m here for it— had a blast!

Very impressed by this super-confident Western take on the kind of content kids over here have loved since the late ‘90s. (Seriously, why did it take so long?) The initial lack of a theatrical release indicates some anxiety over the material, but the last few months have proven that audiences are way into any alternative from the Disney/Pixar house style. Maybe I’d like a few more jokes here and there, but the fact that we have a rare animated film not driven by daddy issues should be celebrated.

author: Unknown
text:
Slick visuals & empty cliches. A good match between style and subject, since the hypercapitalist pop world evolved to present the lack of any veil of authenticity as a feature instead of a bug and everything here from the monsters' design to the mandatory depiction of a panic attack come off as supremely derivative. Yeah, a couple of the songs are catchy, but I don't think I'm being too much of a grouch for wanting a bit more out of big-budget animation.
